description: Sunni Islamist militant group based in Somalia, formed in the mid-2000s as a youth militia within the Islamic Courts Union. It controls territory in south and southwestern Somalia, is allied with al-Qaeda, and has carried out numerous high-fatality attacks in Somalia and neighboring countries. Despite territorial losses after 2011, it remains active with an estimated 7,000–18,000 fighters.

country_links.evidence_quote: On 20 July 2022, Al-Shabaab launched an invasion into Ethiopia's Somali Region . Taking advantage of the ongoing Tigray War , the goal of the operation was to establish a presence for the group within southern Ethiopia .
country_links.context: Al-Shabaab launched an invasion into Ethiopia's Somali Region in July 2022, establishing a presence and advancing into the region.

country_links.evidence_quote: occasionally larger shipments from the Makran coast of Iran
country_links.context: Al-Shabaab receives larger weapons shipments from the Makran coast of Iran.

country_links.evidence_quote: The group undertook high-profile attacks outside Somalia such as the Westgate shopping mall attack by four militants left 67 dead - the deadliest attack in Kenya since the 1998 US embassy bombings in Nairobi . That death-toll was exceeded two years later in the Garissa University College attack in which 148 people were killed in an attack targeting Christian students.
country_links.context: Al-Shabaab carried out high-profile attacks in Kenya, including the Westgate shopping mall attack and the Garissa University College attack.

country_links.evidence_quote: Al-Shabaab, al-Qaeda in Islamic Maghreb (AQIM), and the Nigeria-based Boko Haram were attempting to coordinate their activities
country_links.context: Al-Shabaab attempts to coordinate activities with Nigeria-based Boko Haram.

country_links.evidence_quote: Most of those recruits are from East Africa – primarily Kenya, but also Tanzania and Uganda, all countries in which Al-Shabaab recruitment networks are well entrenched.
country_links.context: Al-Shabaab recruits from Tanzania, indicating a recruitment network there.

country_links.evidence_quote: Most of those recruits are from East Africa – primarily Kenya, but also Tanzania and Uganda, all countries in which Al-Shabaab recruitment networks are well entrenched.
country_links.context: Al-Shabaab recruits from Uganda, indicating a recruitment network there.

country_links.evidence_quote: The flow of recruits from Arab countries and the West has diminished substantially since 2014, except for in the case of Yemen .
country_links.context: Al-Shabaab receives a flow of recruits from Yemen.
